chapters.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years

questions.question: ________ was a fourteenth-century chronicler.
questions.explanation: The fourteenth-century chronicler Ziyauddin Barani wrote his chronicle first in 1356 and another version two years later.

questions.question: Which of the languages was used in Andhra Pradesh according to Amir Khusrau?
questions.explanation: In 1318 the poet Amir Khusrau noted that there was a different language in every region of the land. According to him, the language used in Andhra Pradesh was Telangani. This refers to the Telugu language, which is the official language of the state of Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: A person who makes maps is called ________.
questions.explanation: Cartography is the study and practice of making maps. In this context Cartographer is a person who makes maps.

questions.question: Name the groups who asserted their political importance during the period between 700 to 1750?
questions.explanation: Groups of people such as the Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Ahoms, and Kayasthas (a caste of scribes and secretaries) used the opportunities of the age to become politically important.

questions.question: Who brought the teachings of the Holy Quran to India?
questions.explanation: In the seventh century, Merchants and migrants first brought the teachings of the Holy Quran to India.

questions.question: Which of the following is the holy book of Muslims?
questions.explanation: Muslims regard the Quran as their holy book and accept the sovereignty of the one God, Allah.

questions.question: The periods in which British historians divided Indian history.
questions.explanation: In the middle of the nineteenth century British historians divided the history of India into three periods: “Hindu”, “Muslim” and “British”.

questions.question: ________, a name derived from "Rajaputra" - the son of a Ruler, a body of warriors who claimed Kshatriya caste status.
questions.explanation: One group of people who became important in the medieval period were the Rajputs, a name derived from “Rajaputra”, the son of a ruler. Between the eighth and fourteenth centuries, the term was applied more generally to a body of warriors who claimed Kshatriya caste status.

questions.question: An influential, wealthy individual who supports another person - an artist, a craftsperson, a learned man, or a noble is called _______.
questions.explanation: A person who gives financial or other support to a person, organization, or cause. Hence, with respect to the medieval period, Patron is an influential, wealthy individual who supports another person an artist, a craftsperson, a learned man, or a noble.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the literary source?
questions.explanation: Historians rely upon coins, inscriptions, architecture, and textual records for information.

questions.question: A place where documents and manuscripts are stored as records for knowledge is called an ________.
questions.explanation: An archive is an accumulation of historical records, documents providing information about a place, institution, or group of people. It is a place where documents and manuscripts are stored as records for knowledge.

questions.question: Who made Maps in 1154 CE?
questions.explanation: A section of the world map was drawn by the Arab geographer Al-Idrisi in the twelfth century (1154 CE) showing the Indian subcontinent.

questions.question: Refers to the environment of a region and the social and economic lifestyle of its residents.
questions.explanation: The area or natural environment in which an organism or population normally lives. It refers to the environment of a region and the social and economic lifestyle of its residents.

questions.question: New foods and beverages arrived in the subcontinent were:
questions.explanation: At different moments in the period between 700 to 1750 new foods and beverages made their appearance. For eg. : Arrival of New foods and beverages in the subcontinent: potatoes, corn, chillies, tea, and coffee.

questions.question: Kanauj name is spelled in the Arabic language as:
questions.explanation: In the Arabic language, many words were spelled differently. For Eg: Kanauj's name is a spell as Qanauj.

questions.question: ________ framed their own rules and regulations to manage the conduct of their members.
questions.explanation: As society became more differentiated, people were grouped into jatis or sub-castes and ranked on the basis of their backgrounds and their occupations. Jatis framed their own rules and regulations to manage the conduct of their members.

questions.question: Two styles of writing in Persian and Arabic are:
questions.explanation: Different kinds of handwriting like the Nastaliq style and the Shikaste style could make the reading of Persian and Arabic difficult.

questions.question: Two schools of Islam are:
questions.explanation: There were other important differences between the various schools of law (Hanafi and Shafi’i mainly in India), and in theology and mystic traditions.

questions.question: ________ means the empire crossing its region and spreading over several other regions.
questions.explanation: Pan region empire is a nation which influences other nation's actions completely or partially, generally, a powerful country which has a sphere of influence, might be political or economical or cultural or ideological. Hence, it means the empire crossing its region and spreading over several other regions.

questions.question: Harshavardhana was from:
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana was an Indian emperor who ruled North India from 606 to 647 CE. He was a member of the Gupta dynasty.

questions.question: Siraj used the term Hindustan in:
questions.explanation: Minhaj-i-Siraj used the term "Hindustan" for the first time. He used the term in a political sense for lands that were a part of the dominions of the Delhi Sultan.

questions.question: The worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society were the important changes that occur that we call today.
questions.explanation: The thousand years of history that we are exploring witnessed major developments in religious traditions. It was during this period that important changes occurred in what we call Hinduism today. These included the worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society.

questions.question: Learned theologians and jurists were:
questions.explanation: Many rulers were patrons of Islam and the ulama who were learned, theologians and jurists.

questions.question: Prashasti means:
questions.explanation: Prashasti is a Sanskrit word meaning in praise of King.

questions.question: Who was al-aldrisi?
questions.explanation: Muhammad-Al-Idrisi was a Muslim and Arab cartographer, geographer, traveler.

questions.question: ________ carries with it a sense of material progress and intellectual advancement.
questions.explanation: The “medieval” period is often contrasted with the “modern” period. “Modernity” carries with it a sense of material progress and intellectual advancement.

questions.question: What is the Nastaliq style of writing?
questions.explanation: The Nastaliq style is cursive and easy to read.

questions.question: ________ had knowledge of Sanskrit texts, which made them respectable in the society.
questions.explanation: Brahmanas earned a lot of respect in society because of their knowledge of Sanskrit texts.

questions.question: Who was the ruler of a vast empire that stretched from Bengal (Gauda) in the east to Ghazni (Gajjana) in Afghanistan in the west and included all of south India (Dravida)?
questions.explanation: A Sanskrit prashasti, praising the Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban (1266-87) explained that he was the ruler of a vast empire that stretched from Bengal (Gauda) in the east to Ghazni (Gajjana) in Afghanistan in the west and included all of south India (Dravida).

questions.question: Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban birth and death year is:
questions.explanation: A Sanskrit prashasti praising the Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban birth and death year is 1266-87.

questions.question: Which Mughal ruler used Hindustan to describe the geography, the fauna, and the culture of the inhabitants of the subcontinent?
questions.explanation: In the early sixteenth century Babur used Hindustan to describe the geography, the fauna and the culture of the inhabitants of the subcontinent.

subtopics.name: Thinking about Time and Historical Periods

questions.question: ________ had the support of their patrons, who were new rulers and searching for prestige.
questions.explanation: Brahmanas a lot of respect in society because of knowledge of Sanskrit texts earned. Their dominant position was consolidated by the support of their patrons - new patrons rulers searching for prestige.

questions.question: Why scribes copied manuscripts by hand?

options.option: King banned the use of printing press
It was in fashion to write with hands
It was costly to print the documents

questions.explanation: There was no printing press in those days so scribes copied manuscripts by hand.

questions.question: During the period 700 to 1750 new technologies made their appearance:
questions.explanation: At different moments in the period between - 700 to 1750 new technologies made their appearances - like the Persian wheel in irrigation, the spinning wheel in weaving, and firearms in combat.

questions.question: Hiranyagarbha rituals were performed with the help of
questions.explanation: When Hiranya-garbha (literally, the golden womb) ritual was performed with the help of Brahmanas, it was thought to lead to the “rebirth” of the sacrificer as a Kshatriya, even if he was not one by birth.

questions.question: Kadamba Mayurasharman established their kingdom in
questions.explanation: The Kadamba Mayurasharman was a Brahmana who gave up his traditional profession and took to arms and successfully established his kingdom in Karnataka.

questions.question: Tribhuvana-chakravartin means
questions.explanation: Many of the new kings adopted high-sounding titles like Tribhuvana-chakravartin which means lord of the three worlds.

questions.question: Vetti is a ________.
questions.explanation: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than 400 terms for different kinds of taxes. The most frequently mentioned tax is vetti, taken not in cash but in the form of forced labour.

questions.question: Kadamai means ________.
questions.explanation: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than 400 terms for different kinds of taxes. The most frequently mentioned tax is kadamai

questions.question: The functionaries for collecting revenue were generally recruited from:
questions.explanation: The functionaries for collecting revenue were generally recruited from influential families, and positions were often hereditary.

questions.question: Gwalior is in:
questions.explanation: Gwalior is a city in the central Indian state of Madhya Pradesh.

questions.question: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of ________.
questions.explanation: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land. These were recorded on copper plates, which were given to those who received the land.

questions.question: The specialists who worked at the temple and lived near it were:
questions.explanation: All the specialists who worked at the temple and very often lived near it - priests, garland makers, cooks, sweepers, musicians, dancers, etc.

questions.question: The city of Kanauj is in the
questions.explanation: One of the particularly prized areas was the city of Kanauj in the Ganga valley.

questions.question: Which of the following rulers involved in the tripartite struggles?
questions.explanation: For centuries, rulers belonging to the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties fought for control over Kanauj. Because there were three “parties” in this long-drawn conflict, historians often describe it as the “tripartite struggle”.

questions.question: Mahmud of Ghazni was the ruler of
questions.explanation: In the history, one of the best-known rulers was Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, Afghanistan.

questions.question: Somnath temple is in:
questions.explanation: The Somnath temple located in Prabhas Patan near Veraval in Saurashtra on the western coast of Gujarat.

questions.question: Chola bronze images are considered amongst the finest in the ________.
questions.explanation: Amongst the crafts associated with temples, the making of bronze images was the most distinctive. Chola bronze images are considered amongst the finest in the world.

questions.question: Many of the achievements of the Cholas were made possible through new developments in which field?
questions.explanation: Water from the channels provides the necessary moisture for agriculture, particularly the cultivation of rice.Hence many of the achievements of the Cholas were made possible through new developments in agriculture.

questions.question: The river Kaveri branches off into several small channels before emptying into the:
questions.explanation: The river Kaveri branches off into several small channels before emptying into the Bay of Bengal. These channels overflow frequently, depositing fertile soil on their banks.

questions.question: Chingleput district was in ________.
questions.explanation: Inscriptions from Uttaramerur in Chingleput district, Tamil Nadu, provide details of the way in which the sabha was organised.

questions.question: Many new dynasties emerged after the ________.
questions.explanation: The major ruling dynasties in different parts of the subcontinent between the seventh and twelfth centuries. Many new dynasties emerged after the seventh century.

questions.question: Who were subordinate to the Chalukyas of Karnataka?
questions.explanation: Initially, Rashtrakutas in the Deccan, were subordinate to the Chalukyas of Karnataka.

questions.question: Hiranya-garbha was thought to lead to the "rebirth" of the sacrifice as:
questions.explanation: When a ritual called hiranya-garbha was performed with the help of Brahmanas, it was thought to lead to the “rebirth” of the sacrificer as a Kshatriya, even if he was not one by birth.

questions.question: Gurjara-Pratihara Harichandra established their kingdom in
questions.explanation: The Gurjara-Pratihara Harichandra were Brahmanas who gave up his traditional professions and took to arms and successfully established kingdom Rajasthan.

questions.question: Nagabhata was a ________.
questions.explanation: One prashasti, written in Sanskrit and found in Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, describes the exploits of Nagabhata who is a Pratihara king.

questions.question: Kings rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land and were recorded on
questions.explanation: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land. These were recorded on copper plates, which were given to those who received the land.

questions.question: Kalhana was written in which language?
questions.explanation: Kalhana, a Kashmiri, was the author of Rajatarangini, an account of the history of Kashmir. He wrote his work in Sanskrit.

questions.question: Tripartite struggle fought for control over
questions.explanation: For centuries, rulers belonging to the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties fought for control over Kanauj.

questions.question: Mahmud of Ghazni ruled from
questions.explanation: Extending control over parts of Central Asia, Iran, and the north-western part of the subcontinents Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni ruled from 997 to 1030.

questions.question: Ghazni raided the subcontinent almost every year - his targets were wealthy temples, including that of:
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni extended control over parts of Central Asia, Iran, and the north-western part of the subcontinent. He raided the subcontinent almost every year - his targets were wealthy temples, including that of Somnath, Gujarat.

subtopics.name: A Closer Look: The Cholas-From Uraiyur to Thanjavur

questions.question: Chahamanas attempted to expand their control to the west and the east, where they were opposed by the Chalukyas of:
questions.explanation: Chahamanas, later known as the Chauhans, who ruled over the region around Delhi and Ajmer. They attempted to expand their control to the west and the east, where they were opposed by the Chalukyas of Gujarat.

subtopics.name: A Closer Look: The Cholas-Splendid Temples and Bronze Sculpture

questions.question: Prithviraj birth and death year was-
questions.explanation: The best-known Chahamana ruler Prithviraja III was born in 1168 and died in 1192.

questions.question: Cholas built the town of Thanjavur and a temple for goddess:
questions.explanation: Vijayalaya, who belonged to the ancient chiefly family of the Cholas from Uraiyur, captured the delta from the Muttaraiyar in the middle of the ninth century. He built the town of Thanjavur and a temple for goddess Nishumbhasudini there.

questions.question: In which language was the prashasti found in Gwalior written?
questions.explanation: One prashasti, written in Sanskrit and found in Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, describes the exploits of Nagabhata, a Pratihara king.

subtopics.name: A Closer Look: The Cholas-Agriculture and Irrigation

questions.question: What is the literal meaning of Gangaikonda?
questions.explanation: Gangaikonda means the town of the Chola who took over(conquered)Ganga (water from Ganga).

questions.question: Name the Brahmanas who gave up their traditional professions and took to arms.
questions.explanation: The Kadamba Mayurasharman and the Gurjara-Pratihara Harichandra were Brahmanas who gave up their traditional professions and took to arms, successfully establishing kingdoms in Karnataka and Rajasthan respectively.

subtopics.name: A Closer Look: The Cholas-The Administration of the Empire

questions.question: What is the significance of the ring?

options.option: The ring holding the plates together is secured with the royal seal, to indicate that this is an authentic document
The ring holding the plates together is secured with the royal seal, to indicate that this is an illegal document
The ring holding the plates together is unsecured with the royal seal, to indicate that this is an authentic document
The ring holding the plates together is unsecured with the farmer seal, to indicate that this is an authentic document

questions.explanation: The set of copper plates recording a grant of land made by a ruler in the ninth century. The ring holding the plates together is secured with the royal seal, to indicate that this is an authentic document.

subtopics.name: Finding Out about the Delhi Sultans

questions.question: The duration of Khalji dynasty is:
questions.explanation: The Khalji or Khilji dynasty was a Muslim dynasty which ruled large parts of the Indian subcontinent between 1290 and 1320.

questions.question: Who was the famous invader of Mughals?
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan or Chinggis Khaan was the founder and first Great Khan of the Mongol Empire and was the famous invader of Mughals.

questions.question: Ibn Batuta was a fourteenth Century traveller from
questions.explanation: Ibn Batutah(1304-1369), was a Moroccan(Northern Africa)- a scholar who widely travelled the medieval world. Ibn Battuta visited most of the Islamic world and many non-Muslim lands, including North Africa, the Horn of Africa, West Africa, the Middle East, India, Central Asia, South east Asia, and China. He dictated an account of his journeys, titled-A Gift to Those Who Contemplate the Wonders of Cities and the Marvels of Travelling. This account of his journeys provides a picture of medieval civilization that is still widely consulted today.

questions.question: Warangal is in:
questions.explanation: Warangal is a city in the south Indian state of Telangana. It was the capital of the Kakatiya dynasty from the 12th to 14th centuries, and many intricately carved monuments from this period still stand. Among them are the ruins of 13th-century Warangal Fort and the Thousand Pillar Temple, built-in 1163.

questions.question: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the ________.
questions.explanation: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the Tomara Rajputs. Angapal of the Tomar Dynasty built a fort called Lal Kot (the present area around Suraj Kund, Delhi). Later on, Chauhans defeated Tomaras and established their rule.

questions.question: Tomara Rajputs were defeated in the middle of the twelfth century by the Chauhans of:
questions.explanation: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the Tomara Rajputs, who were defeated in the middle of the twelfth century by the Chauhans (also referred to as Chahamanas) of Ajmer. It was under the Tomaras and Chauhans that Delhi became an important commercial centre. Many rich Jaina merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples. Coins minted here, called Dehliwal, had a wide circulation.

questions.question: Which of the following is not from the Rajput dynasty?
questions.explanation: Raziya Begum sultan name, Raziya al-Din (1205 - 1240), throne name Jalalat-ud-Din Raziya, usually referred to in history as Razia Sultan, was the Muslim Sultan of Delhi from 10 November 1236 - 14 October 1240. She was famously the only female ever to rule the Delhi Sultanate.

questions.question: What is the time of rule of Ananga Pala?
questions.explanation: Anangpal Tomar ruled over the areas of Delhi in India. In the 11th century, Anangapala Tomar ruled from 1130 to 1145. Little is known of Tomar, who was a Gurjar from the Tomara dynasty and whose ancestors had settled in the Aravalli Hills around the end of the first millennium CE.

questions.question: Raziyya was removed from the throne in ________.
questions.explanation: Raziyya Sultana was the daughter of Shams ud din Iltutmish, who had begun life as a Turk slave and ended it as Sultan of Delhi. In 1236 Sultan Iltutmish’s daughter Raziyya became Sultan. Minhaj-i Siraj recognised that she was more able and qualified than all her brothers. She was removed from the throne in 1240.

questions.question: Which of the following city was not developed by Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: The transformation of Delhi into a capital that controlled vast areas of the subcontinent started with the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate at the beginning of the thirteenth century. The Delhi Sultans built many cities in the area that we now know as Delhi. These were Dehli-i-Kuhna, Siri, and Jahanpanah.

questions.question: Sher Shah Suri defeated the Mughal emperor:
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Sur (1540-1545) started his career as the manager of a small territory for his uncle in Bihar and eventually challenged and defeated the Mughal emperor Humayun (1530-1540, 1555-1556). Sher Shah captured Delhi and established his own dynasty. Although the Suri dynasty ruled for only fifteen years (1540-1555), it introduced an administration that borrowed elements from Alauddin Khalji and made them more efficient.

questions.question: Suri dynasty ruled for only ________.
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1540-1545) started his career as the manager of a small territory in Bihar and eventually challenged and defeated the Mughal emperor Humayun (1530-1540, 1555-1556). Sher Shah captured Delhi and established his own dynasty. Although the Suri dynasty ruled for only fifteen years (1540-1555), it introduced an administration that borrowed elements from Alauddin Khalji. Sher Shah’s administration became the model followed by the great emperor Akbar.

subtopics.name: A Closer Look: Administration and Consolidation under the Khaljis and Tughluqs

questions.question: The idea of the Three Orders was first formulated in-
questions.explanation: The “Three Orders” were the “Peace of God”, Knights and the Crusades. The idea of the “Three Orders” was first formulated in France in the early eleventh century. It divided society into three classes: those who prayed, those who fought, and those who tilled the land. This division of society into “Three Orders” was supported by the Church to consolidate its dominant role in society. This helped the emergence of a new warrior group called knights.

questions.question: The authors of tawarikh were learned men were:
questions.explanation: Tarikh (singular) / Tawarikh (plural), written in Persian, was the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans. The authors of tawarikh were learned men: secretaries, administrators, poets, and courtiers, who both recounted events and advised rulers on governance, emphasizing the importance of just rule.

questions.question: What forced the two rulers Allauddin Khilji and Muhammed Tughluq to mobilise a large standing army in Delhi?
questions.explanation: The Mongols under Genghis Khan invaded Transoxiana in north-east Iran in 1219 and the Delhi Sultanate faced their onslaught soon after. Mongol attacks on the Delhi Sultanate increased during the reign of Alauddin Khalji and in the early years of Muhammad Tughluq’s rule. This forced the two rulers to mobilise a large standing army in Delhi which posed a huge administrative challenge.

questions.question: ________, a fourteenth-century chronicler reported the appointments as a sign of the sultan's loss of judgment and his incapacity to rule.
questions.explanation: Ziauddin Barani, a mid-fourteenth-century-chronicler, reported the appointments as a sign of the Sultan’s loss of political judgment and his incapacity to rule.

questions.question: Where were the residents of the city of Dehli-i-Kuhna sent?
questions.explanation: The Sultanate was attacked in the early years of Muhammad Tughluq’s reign. The Mongol army was defeated. Muhammad Tughluq was confident about the strength of his army and his resources to plan an attack on Transoxiana. He, therefore, raised a large standing army. Rather than constructing a new garrison town, the oldest of the four cities of Delhi (Dehli-i Kuhna) was emptied of its residents and the soldiers garrisoned there. The residents of the old city were sent to the new capital of Daulatabad in the south.

subtopics.name: The Sultanate in the Fifteenth and Sixteenth Centuries

questions.question: The five dynasties that together made the Delhi Sultanate were:
questions.explanation: The Delhi Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate based mostly in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for 320 years (1206-1526). Five dynasties ruled over the Delhi Sultanate sequentially: the Mamluk dynasty (1206-90), the Khalji dynasty (1290-1320), the Tughlaq dynasty (1320-1414), the Sayyid dynasty (1414-51) and the Lodi dynasty (1451-1526).

questions.question: Which Phrase from the following defined the relationship between the king and his subjects?
questions.explanation: The Circle of Justice: Fakhr-i Mudabbir wrote in the thirteenth century. A king cannot survive without soldiers. And soldiers cannot live without salaries. Salaries come from the revenue collected from peasants. But peasants can pay revenue only when they are prosperous and happy. This happens when the king promotes justice and honest governance.

questions.question: Sher-Shah Suri was killed in ________.
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1486-22 May 1545), born Farid Khan was the founder of the Sur Empire in North India, with its capital at Delhi. Sher Shah took control of the Mughal Empire in 1540. Sher Shah was killed on 22 May 1545 during the siege of the Kalinjar fort of Rajputs.

questions.question: What was the official language of Mughal empire?
questions.explanation: Nobody was forced to learn any particular language at the time of Mughal sultanate but the official language was Persian. The language of the court was also Persian, the business of the court was conducted in Persian. Those documents are still intact. Lots of those documents are now translated into English. People who study Mughal history oftentimes learn Persian also to dig out information from original Persian documents.

questions.question: Who was the first Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: Babur was the first Mughal emperor (1526 - 1530), who succeeded the throne of Ferghana in 1494 when he was only 12 years old. He was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs. After years of wandering, he seized Kabul in 1504. In 1526 he defeated the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi, at Panipat and captured Delhi and Agra.

questions.question: Amber is in ________.
questions.explanation: Jaipur State was a princely state of India from 1128 to 1948. It was centred on Jaipur town. It existed from the 12th century until Indian Independence in 1947. According to the different periods of history it was also known as Jaipur Kingdom, Amber Kingdom, Dhundhar Kingdom, and Kachwaha Kingdom.

questions.question: When did Humayun re-establish Mughal empire in India?
questions.explanation: Sher Khan defeated Humayun at Chausa (1539) and Kanauj (1540), forcing him to flee to Iran. In Iran, Humayun received help from the Safavid Shah. He recaptured Delhi in 1555 but died in an accident in the following year.

questions.question: ________ refused to accept the Mughal authority for a long time.
questions.explanation: The Sisodiya Rajputs refused to accept Mughal authority for a long time. Once defeated, however, they were honourably treated by the Mughals, given their lands (Watan) back as assignments (watan jagir). The careful balance between defeating but not humiliating their opponents enabled the Mughals to extend their influence over many kings and chieftains.

questions.question: Who wrote Ain-i-Akbari?
questions.explanation: The Ain-i-Akbari or the "Constitution of Akbar", is a 16th-century, detailed document recording the administration of emperor Akbar's empire, written by his vizier, Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak. It makes the Volume III and the final part of the much larger document, the Akbarnama, the Book of Akbar, also by Abul Fazl, and it itself is in three volumes.

questions.question: From their father's side, Mughals were the successors of:
questions.explanation: The Mughals were descendants of two great lineages of rulers. From their mother’s side, they were descendants of Genghis Khan, ruler of the Mongol tribes, China and Central Asia. From their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq, and modern-day Turkey.

questions.question: The rule of primogeniture is:

options.option: Where the youngest daughter inherited his father's estate
Where the youngest son inherited his father's estate
Where the eldest son inherited his father's estate
Where the eldest daughter inherited his father's estate

questions.explanation: Primogeniture is the right of succession belonging to the firstborn child, especially the feudal rule by which the whole real estate of an intestate passed to the eldest son. The Mughals did not believe in the rule of primogeniture. Instead, they followed the Mughal and Timurid custom of coparcenary inheritance or a division of the inheritance amongst all the sons.

questions.question: Who insulted Shivaji when he came to accept Mughal authority?
questions.explanation: Mughal intervention in the succession and internal politics of the Rathor Rajputs of Marwar led to their rebellion. Campaigns against the Maratha chieftain Shivaji were initially successful. But Aurangzeb insulted Shivaji who escaped from Agra, declared himself an independent king and resumed his campaigns against the Mughals.

questions.question: What does the term Mansab mean?
questions.explanation: Those who joined Mughal service were enrolled as mansabdars. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities. The term mansab means a position or rank, and Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a mansab.

questions.question: In Mansabdar system rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called?
questions.explanation: Those who joined Mughal service were enrolled as Mansabdars. The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix: rank, salary and military responsibilities. Rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called Zat.

questions.question: Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments known as-
questions.explanation: The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities. Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments called jagirs which were somewhat like Iqtas.

questions.question: Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices and areas cultivated for a ten-year period of:
questions.explanation: The main source of income available to Mughal rulers was the tax on the produce of the peasantry. Each province was divided into revenue circles with its own schedule of revenue rates for individual crops. This revenue system was known as Zabt. Akbar’s revenue minister, Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices, and areas cultivated for a ten-year period, 1570-1580. On the basis of this data, the tax was fixed on each crop in cash.

questions.question: Who helped Babur against Lodhis?
questions.explanation: Rana Sanga invited Babur with a cunning plan of letting him destroy Lodhis of Delhi.

questions.question: Elizabeth was the last ruler of a dynasty known as the ________.
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth I (1558-1603) of England was the last ruler of a dynasty known as the Tudors. Elizabeth’s rule was marked by several conflicts - foremost amongst these were conflicts between the Roman Catholic Church and the Protestants, who were attempting to reform the Church.

questions.question: When was Queen Elizabeth I born and died?
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth, I born on 7 September 1533  and died on 24 March 1603 was Queen of England and Ireland from 17 November 1558 until her death. Sometimes called The Good Queen Bess, Elizabeth was the last monarch of the House of Tudors. Elizabeth’s rule was marked by several conflicts - foremost amongst these were conflicts between the Roman Catholic Church and the Protestants, who were attempting to reform the Church.

questions.question: A poet named Edmund Spenser wrote a long epic poem called:
questions.explanation: A poet named Edmund Spenser wrote a long epic poem called The Faerie Queene in her praise.

questions.question: Who among the following is not the son of Shah Jahan?
questions.explanation: Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram better known by his regnal name Shah Jahan was the fifth Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1628 to 1658. In 1657-1658, there was conflict over succession amongst Shah Jahan’s 7 sons: Aurangzeb Dara Shukoh, Shah Shauja, Murad Baksh Sultan Daulat Afza, Sultan Luftallah, Sultan Ummid Baksh, Aurangzeb was victorious and acceded the throne.

questions.question: Salim was the name of Mughal emperor-
questions.explanation: Mirza Nur-ud-din Beig Mohammad Khan Salim, or "Salim" known by his imperial name Jahangir (31 August 1569 - 28 October 1627), was the fourth Mughal Emperor who ruled from 1605 until his death in 1627.

questions.question: The capital of Mirza Hakim, Akbar's half-brother, was ________.
questions.explanation: Mirza Muhammad Hakim (29 April 1553 – 10 October 1585), sometimes known simply as Mirza Hakim, or Mirza, was the second son of Mughal emperor Humayun. He ruled Kabul in Afghanistan and often conflicted with his elder brother emperor Akbar. Mirza Hakim, later on, mended ways with Emperor Akbar. He is known for writing Tajdar-e-Haram.

questions.question: If Zat determined a Mansabdar's rank and salary, then sawar indicated his ________.
questions.explanation: The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. Rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called Zat. The Mansabdar’s military responsibilities required him to maintain a specified number of sawar or cavalrymen. The Mansabdar brought his cavalrymen for review, got them registered, their horses branded, and then received money to pay them as salary.

subtopics.name: Mughal Relations with Other Rulers-Mansabdars and Jagirdars

questions.question: Who lost the second battle of Panipat?
questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Panipat was fought on November 5, 1556, between the forces of Hemu, the Hindu general and Chief Minister of Adil Shah Suri, and the army of the Mughal emperor, Akbar. When Hemu Vikramaditya at the battle of Panipat attacked Akbar at the head of his army it was the artillery he had lost that was utilized by the enemy to hold on to its ranks.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation:
The Delhi Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate based mostly in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for 320 years (1206-1526). Five dynasties ruled over the Delhi Sultanate sequentially:

  1. the Mamluk dynasty (1206-90),
  2. the Khalji dynasty (1290-1320),
  3. theTughlaq dynasty (1320-1414),
  4. the Sayyid dynasty (1414-51), and
  5. the Lodi dynasty (1451-1526): The last ruler of this dynasty, Ibrahim Lodi was defeated and killed by Babur in the First Battle of Panipat on April 20, 1526. After Defeated by Babur in the First battle of Panipat (in 1526)thus the Delhi Sultanate ended in 1526.

questions.question: Who lost the fierce battle of Khanwa?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Khanwa was fought near the village of Khanwa, in Bharatpur District of Rajasthan, on March 17, 1527. It was the second major battle fought in modern-day India, between the invading forces of the first Mughal Emperor Babur and the Rajput forces led by Rana Sanga of Mewar, after the Battle of Panipat. Rana Sanga lost the battle. The victory Of Babur's Army consolidated the new Mughal dynasty in India.

questions.question: Who wrote the biography of Humayun?
questions.explanation: Gulbadan Begum (1523 - 7 February 1603) was a Mughal princess and the youngest daughter of Emperor Babur, the founder of the Mughal Empire and the first Mughal emperor. She is best known as the author of Humayun-Nama, the account of the life of her half-brother, Emperor Humayun, which she wrote on the request of her nephew, Emperor Akbar.

questions.question: Rana Sanga was the king of which place?
questions.explanation: Maharana Sangram Singh (12 April 1482 - 30 January 1528) commonly known as Rana Sanga, was Rana of Mewar and head of a powerful Hindu Rajput confederacy in Rajputana during the 16th century. He belonged to the Sisodiya clan of Rajput. Rana ruled between 1508 and 1528. Rana Sanga succeeded his father, Rana Raimal, as king of Mewar in 1508. He fought against the Afghan Lodhi dynasty of Delhi Sultanate, and later against the Mughals.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of Mughal Empire?
questions.explanation: Mirza Abu Zafar Sirajuddin Muhammad Bahadur Shah Zafar or Bahadur Shah Zafar II, (24 October 1775 - 7 November 1862) was the last Mughal emperor. He was the second son and became the successor to his father, Akbar II, upon his death on 28 September 1837. He was a nominal Emperor, as the Mughal Empire existed in name only, and his authority was limited only to the city of Delhi.

questions.question: When did Jahangir die?
questions.explanation: Mirza Nur-ud-din Beig Mohammad Khan Salim, known by his imperial name Jahangir (31 August 1569 - 28 October 1627) was the fourth Mughal Emperor who ruled from 1605 until his death in 1627. Jahangir died in 1627 due to excessive drinking.

subtopics.name: Mughal Relations with Other Rulers-Zabt and Zamindars

questions.question: Babur used Cannons effectively at the first battle of ________.
questions.explanation: Cannons were an important addition in sixteenth-century warfare. Babur used them effectively at the first battle of Panipat. Babur's artillery played a "key role" in the establishment of the Mughal Empire. In 1526, the First Battle of Panipat saw the introduction of massed artillery tactics to Indian warfare. Under the guidance of Ottoman gun master Ustad Ali Quli, Babur deployed cannons behind a screening row of carts.

questions.question: Aurangzeb was the son of Emperor ________.
questions.explanation: Muhi-ud-Din Muhammad commonly known by the sobriquet Aurangzeb was born on 3 November 1618, in Dahod, Gujarat. He was the third son and sixth child of Emperor Shah Jahan and Mumtaz Mahal. He was the sixth, and widely considered the last effective Mughal emperor. His reign lasted for 49 years from 1658 until his death in 1707.

questions.question: When was the first battle of Panipat fought?
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat, on 21 April 1526, was fought between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi Kingdom. It took place in north India and marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire. This was one of the earliest battles involving gunpowder firearms and field artillery in the Indian subcontinent which were introduced by Mughals in this battle.

questions.question: Who was the revenue minister in the court of Emperor Akbar?
questions.explanation: Todar Mal was the Revenue or Finance Minister of the Mughal empire during Akbar's reign. He was one of the Navaratnas in Akbar's durbar.

subtopics.name: The Mughal Empire in the Seventeenth Century and After

questions.question: Who was prince Khurram?
questions.explanation: Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram widely famous as Shah Jahan was born on 5 January 1592 was the third son of Prince Jahangir. The name "Khurram" (joyous) was chosen for the young prince by his grandfather, Emperor Akbar, with whom the young prince shared a close relationship.

questions.question: The book which was written by Abul Fazl is divided into how many volumes?
questions.explanation:
The Akbarnama which translates to Book of Akbar is the official chronicle of the reign of Akbar, written by his court historian and biographer, Abul Fazl who was one of the nine jewels in Akbar's court. It has 3 volumes,

  1. The first volume of Akbarnama deals with the birth of Akbar, the history of Timur's family, and the reigns of Babur and Humayun and the Suri sultans of Delhi.
  2. The second volume describes the detailed history of the reign of Akbar till 1602 and records the events during Akbar's reign. It also deals with that how Bairam Khan and Akbar won the battle of Panipat against Hemu an Indian warrior.
  3. The third volume is named Ain-a-Akbari and details the administrative system of the Empire.

questions.question: Tribes obtained their livelihood in various ways except which of the following?
questions.explanation: Many tribes obtained their livelihood from agriculture. Others were hunter-gatherers or herders.

questions.question: ________ refers to a group of families or households claiming descent from a common ancestor.
questions.explanation: A clan is a group of families or households claiming descent from a common ancestor. The tribal organization is often based on kinship or clan loyalties.

questions.question: In which part, the shepherd tribe of Gaddis lived?
questions.explanation: Shepherd tribe of Gaddis lived in the Western Himalaya.

questions.question: Mundas and Santals lived in the region of:
questions.explanation: The Mundas and Santals were among the other important tribes that lived in this region and also in Orissa and Bengal.

questions.question: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across:
questions.explanation: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across western and central India. By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become settled agriculturists and some even zamindars. Many Bhil clans, nevertheless, remained hunter-gatherers.

questions.question: Bhuiyans means:
questions.explanation: Bhuiyans means landlords.

questions.question: Kabui tribe live in which of the following state?
questions.explanation: The 33 scheduled tribes of Manipur are Aimol, Anal, Angami, Chiru, Chothe, Gangte, Hmar, Kabui, Kacha Naga, Koirao, Koireng, Kom, Lamkang, Mao, Maram, Maring, Any Mizo tribe (Lushei), Monsang, Moyon, Paite, Purum, Ralte, Sema, Simte, Sahlte (Sukte), Tangkhul, Thadou, Vaiphei, Zou, Kharam, Tarao, Poumai Naga, and Any Kuki tribe.

questions.question: Following are the tribes of east India except:
questions.explanation: The Langah is a Muslim tribe and is settled Punjab, Sindh, Balochistan, Lasbela in Pakistan, and in the states of Gujarat and Rajasthan in India.

questions.question: The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to:
questions.explanation: The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to Kolis, Berads and numerous others.

questions.question: Which of the following is tribe of the South region?
questions.explanation: Further south there were large tribal populations of Koragas, Vetars, Maravars, and many others.

questions.question: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across western and central India. By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become:

options.option: Started making pottery and some even zamindars
Started making pottery and some even craftsman

questions.explanation: By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become settled agriculturists and some even zamindars.

questions.question: Who used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets?
questions.explanation: Sultan Alauddin Khalji used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets.

questions.question: Who transported food grain for the Mughals army during military campaigns?
questions.explanation: Banjaras transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be 100,000 bullocks carrying grain.

questions.question: ________ rather than varnas became the basis for organising society.
questions.explanation: Jatis, rather than varna, became the basis for organising society.

questions.question: Rajput belongs to different lineages except which one of them?
questions.explanation: They belonged to different lineages, such as Hunas, Chandelas, Chalukyas and others.

questions.question: Salbahan was the:
questions.explanation: Salbahan was the Chandel Rajput raja of Mahoba.

questions.question: Who was the son of Durgawati and Dalpat?
questions.explanation: Dalpat, however, died early. Rani Durgawati was very capable and started ruling on behalf of her five-year-old son, Bir Narain.

questions.question: In which year the Mughals forces under Asaf Khan attacked Garha Katanga?
questions.explanation: In 1565, the Mughal forces under Asaf Khan attacked Garha Katanga. Strong resistance was put up by Rani Durgawati. She was defeated and preferred to die rather than surrender.

questions.question: In 1662 who defeated the Ahom kingdom?
questions.explanation: In 1662, the Mughals under Mir Jumla attacked the Ahom kingdom. Despite their brave defence, the Ahoms were defeated. But direct Mughal control over the region could not last long.

questions.question: The ear ornament figure has been taken from which tribe?
questions.explanation: Ear ornaments, made by Koboi Naga tribe, Manipur.

questions.question: The Gonds were founding great numbers across the present-day states of:

options.option: Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh
Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh

questions.explanation: The Gonds were found in great numbers across the present-day states of Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: Who replaced the older rulers, especially in agricultural areas?
questions.explanation: Among the Kshatriyas, new Rajput clans became powerful by the eleventh and twelfth centuries. They belonged to different lineages, such as Hunas, Chandelas, Chalukyas, and others. Some of these, too, had been tribes earlier. Many of these clans came to be regarded as Rajputs. They gradually replaced the older rulers, especially in agricultural areas.

questions.question: Banjaras transported food grain for the Mughals army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be:
questions.explanation: They transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be 100,000 bullocks carrying grain.

questions.question: Who described about the Banjaras?
questions.explanation: Peter Mundy, an English trader who came to India during the early seventeenth century, has described the Banjaras: In the morning we met a tanda of Banjaras with14,000 oxen. They were all laden with grains such as wheat and rice. These Banjaras carry their household - wives, and children – along with them. One tanda consists of many families. Their way of life is similar to that of carriers who continuously travel from place to place. They own their oxen. They are sometimes hired by merchants, but most commonly they are themselves, merchants. They buy grain where it is cheaply available and carry it to places where it is dearer. From there, they again reload their oxen with anything that can be profitably sold in other places. In a tanda, there may be as many as 6 or 7 hundred persons. They do not travel more than 6 or 7 miles a day - that, too, in the cool weather. After unloading their oxen, they turn them free to graze as there is enough land here, and no one there to forbid them.

questions.question: By 1206 who had united the Mongol and Turkish tribes into a powerful military force?
questions.explanation: By 1206 Genghis Khan had united the Mongol and Turkish tribes into a powerful military force.

questions.question: Shankra was an advocate of-
questions.explanation: Shankara one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the eighth century. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality.

questions.question: Which deities were came to be worshipped through elaborate rituals?
questions.explanation: Shiva, Vishnu, and Durga as supreme deities came to be worshipped through elaborate rituals.

questions.question: Nayanars were:
questions.explanation: Nayanars were (saints devoted to Shiva)

questions.question: Hagiography means:
questions.explanation: Hagiography means writing of saint's lives.

questions.question: Who was born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century and was deeply influenced by the Alvars?
questions.explanation: Ramanuja, born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century, was deeply influenced by the Alvars. According to him the best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu.

questions.question: Which amongst the following are the saints of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste.

questions.question: Which temple is located in Pandharpur?
questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste. This regional tradition of bhakti focused on the Vitthala (a form of Vishnu) temple in Pandharpur.

questions.question: Who amongst the following was not the Sikh Guru?
questions.explanation: Before his death in 1539, Guru Nanak appointed one of his followers as his successor. His name was Lehna but he came to be known as Guru Angad, signifying that he was a part of Guru Nanak himself. Guru Angad compiled the compositions of Guru Nanak, to which he added his own in a new script known as Gurmukhi. The three successors of Guru Angad also wrote under the name of “Nanak” and all of their compositions were compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604. To this compilation were added the writings of other figures like Shaikh Farid, Sant Kabir, Bhagat Namdev, and Guru Tegh Bahadur. In 1706 this compilation was authenticated by his son and successor, Guru Gobind Singh. It is now known as Guru Granth Sahib, the holy scripture of the Sikhs.

questions.question: Shankaradeva composed poems and plays in which language?
questions.explanation: Surdas was an ardent devotee of Krishna. His compositions, compiled in the Sursagara, Surasaravali, and Sahitya Lahari, express his devotion. Also contemporary was Shankaradeva of Assam (late fifteenth century) who emphasised devotion to Vishnu, and composed poems and plays in the Assamese.

questions.question: Whose bhajan are preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, Panch Vani, and Bijak?
questions.explanation: Kabir, who probably lived in the fifteenth-sixteenth centuries, was one of the most influential saints. We have little reliable information about his life. We get to know of his ideas from a vast collection of verses called Sakhis and pads said to have been composed by him and sung by wandering bhajan singers. Some of these were later collected and preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, Panch Vani, and Bijak.

questions.question: The Sikh movement began to get politicized in the seventeenth century, a development which culminated in the institution of the Khalsa by:
questions.explanation: The Sikh movement began to get politicized in the seventeenth century, a development which culminated in the institution of the Khalsa by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699. The community of the Sikhs, called the Khalsa Panth, became a political entity.

questions.question: Which of the following is an Alvar saint?
questions.explanation: There were 12 Alvars, who came from equally divergent backgrounds, the best known being Periyalvar, his daughter Andal, Tondaradippodi Alvar, and Nammalvar. Their songs were compiled in the Divya Prabandham.

questions.question: Who propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita?
questions.explanation: Ramanuja, born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century, was deeply influenced by the Alvars. According to him the best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu. Vishnu in His grace helps the devotee to attain the bliss of union with Him. He propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita or qualified oneness in that the soul even when united with the Supreme God remained distinct.

questions.question: Who rejected all forms of ritualism, outward display of piety, and social differences based on birth? In fact, they even rejected the idea of renunciation and preferred to live with their families, earning their livelihood like any other person.
questions.explanation: The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste. These saint-poets rejected all forms of ritualism, outward display of piety, and social differences based on birth. In fact, they even rejected the idea of renunciation and preferred to live with their families, earning their livelihood like any other person, while humbly serving fellow human beings in need.

questions.question: Who propagated strict monotheism or submission to one God. It also rejected idol worship and considerably simplified rituals of worship into collective prayers?
questions.explanation: Islam propagated strict monotheism or submission to one God. It also rejected idol worship and considerably simplified rituals of worship into collective prayers.

questions.question: To which century and state did Chaitanyadeva belong?
questions.explanation: Chaitanyadeva, a sixteenth-century bhakti saint from Bengal, preached selfless devotion to Krishna-Radha. In the picture you see a group of his followers engaged in ecstatic dancing and singing.

questions.question: Where was Guru Nanak born?
questions.explanation: Guru Nanak (1469-1539) than about Kabir. Born at Talwandi (Nankana Sahib in Pakistan), he travelled widely before establishing a centre at Kartarpur (Dera Baba Nanak on the river Ravi)

questions.question: Whose songs became popular with the masses in Rajasthan and Gujarat?
questions.explanation: Mirabai was a Rajput princess married into the royal family of Mewar in the sixteenth century. Mirabai became a disciple of Ravidas, a saint from a caste considered “untouchable”. She was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion. Her songs also openly challenged the norms of the “upper” castes and became popular with the masses in Rajasthan and Gujarat.

questions.question: Who amongst the following belongs to the Mahar caste family?
questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. questions.question: Which Mughal ordered the execution of Guru Arjan in 1606?
questions.explanation: The Mughal emperor Jahangir looked upon them as a potential threat and he ordered the execution of Guru Arjan in 1606. questions.question: Who rewrote the Gita in Marathi?
questions.explanation: Bhagavad Gita is part of Mahabharata written by Veda Vyasa Dnyaneshwar (1275-1296 CE) translated and commented on the Gita in Marathi, in his book Dnyaneshwari.

questions.question:
Which of the following included in the philosophy of Shankara

  1. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul
  2. He propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita
  3. He considered the world around us to be an illusion or maya,
  4. The best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu
questions.explanation: Shankara, one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the eighth century. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality. He taught that Brahman, the only or Ultimate Reality, was formless and without any attributes. He considered the world around us to be an illusion or maya, and preached renunciation of the world and adoption of the path of knowledge to understand the true nature of Brahman and attain salvation.

questions.question: Nathpanthis, Siddhas and Yogis believe in the following ideas-

options.option: To them, the path to salvation lay in meditation on the formless Ultimate Reality and the realisation of oneness with
They advocated intense training of the mind and body through practices like yogasnas, breathing exercises, and meditation

questions.explanation: Nathpanthis, Siddhacharas and Yogis. They advocated renunciation of the world. To them, the path to salvation lay in meditation on the formless Ultimate Reality and the realisation of oneness with it. To achieve this they advocated intense training of the mind and body through practices like yogasanas, breathing exercises, and meditation. These groups became particularly popular among “low” castes. Their criticism of conventional religion created the ground for devotional religion to become a popular force in northern India.

questions.question: The script of Sikhs is_______.
questions.explanation: Gurmukhi is a Sikh script modified, standardised and used by the second Sikh Guru, Guru Angad (1563 – 1606).

questions.question: Jaipur is in which state?
questions.explanation: Jaipur is the capital of India’s Rajasthan state.

questions.question: Attribution of living soul to plants, inanimate objects, and natural phenomena-
questions.explanation: Animism means the belief that all natural things, such as plants, animals, rocks, and thunder, have spirits and can influence human events.

questions.question: In 1230, Who dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the deputy of the god
questions.explanation: In 1230, King Anangabhima III dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the "deputy" of the god.

questions.question: Which state was called Rajputana by the British?
questions.explanation: Rajputana was a princely state under the British rule now a Rajasthan state.

questions.question: Kathak was developed in two traditions or gharanas:

options.option: In the courts of Rajasthan and the other in Kerala
In the courts of Rajasthan and the other in Lucknow
In the courts of Kerala and the other in Lucknow
In the courts of Gujarat and the other in Orissa

questions.explanation: Under the Mughal emperors and their nobles, Kathak was performed in the court, where it acquired its present features and developed into a form of dance with a distinctive style. Subsequently, it developed in two traditions or gharanas: one in the courts of Rajasthan (Jaipur) and the other in Lucknow.

questions.question: Which language was used in Hungary before the nineteenth century as an official language?

Many part of the environment are already secured by the excess use of resources
Most of the human activities protect the environment like deforestation

questions.explanation: An ecosystem is all the plants and animals that live in a particular area together with the complex relationship that exists between them and their environment. The biotic and abiotic components interact through nutrient cycles and energy flows.

questions.question: Which domain protects us from the harmful rays and scorching heat of the sun?

It is a part of Mantle which is the layer over the earth's surface

The uppermost layer over the earth's surface

questions.explanation: The oceanic crust is mainly called sima.

questions.explanation: The core is having a radius of about 3500 km.

questions.explanation: Sediment is a naturally occurring material that is broken down by processes of weathering and erosion, and is subsequently transported by the action of wind, water, or ice, and/or by the force of gravity acting on the particles.

questions.question: The rock which is made up of molten magma is

questions.question: What happens when igneous and sedimentary rocks go under heat and pressure?

questions.explanation: Rock sediments are transported and deposited by water.

questions.question: It lies below the mantle and around the centre of the earth. What is it?
questions.explanation: The Earth's inner core is the Earth's innermost part. It is composed of an iron-nickel alloy and some other elements. Layers of earth are core, mantle and crust. So, core lies below mantle and is around the centre of the earth.

questions.question: The deepest mine in the world is in ________.

questions.question: Why igneous rocks are called basic rocks?

options.option: These are first to be formed in the rock cycle
These are found on the base of the earth

questions.explanation: As igneous rocks are formed from magma and begin the rock cycle, they are called primary rocks of basic rocks. All other rocks are derived from them, which is why you will also find them referred to as parent rocks on occasion. So, igneous rocks are called basic rocks.

questions.question: The main mineral constituents of the continental mass called ________.

questions.question: Sima stands for

questions.question: The Innermost layer of the earth is made up of ________.
questions.explanation: The innermost layer is the core with a radius of about 3500 km. It is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called nife (ni - nickel and - fe - ferrous i.e. iron). The central core has a very high temperature and pressure. Thus, the innermost layer of the earth is made up of nife.

questions.question: Any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth's crust is called ________.

  4. They are formed of molten lava.

questions.question: Greek word metamorphose means:

questions.question: Rocks which contains fossils are

It is made up of silicon and ferrous
It has a very high temperature and pressure

questions.question: How limestone is changed into marble?

Under great heat and pressure the limestone changes into marble
By putting the limestone in the fire it changes into marble

questions.question: Igneous rocks have:
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks have pure minerals.

questions.question: Rocks used for building materials are

questions.question: The radius of the earth is ________.

questions.question: Gold, petroleum and coal are examples of:

questions.question: When the wind blows, it lifts and transports sand from one place to another. When it stops blowing the sand falls and gets deposited in low hill like structures. These are called ________.

These are called sand dunes. When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances.

questions.question: Large deposits of loess is found in
questions.explanation: When the grains of sand are very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called loess. Large deposits of loess is found in China. Can be understood with the picture given below:

questions.question: Which of the following is not the coastal landforms?
questions.explanation: When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called loess. Thus, loess is a desert landform and all others are coastal landforms.

questions.question: Which of the following river form delta?

questions.question: Where are the Victoria Falls located?
