questions.question: What is Weathering?

options.option: It is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth's surface
It is the wearing away of the landscape by wind
It is the wearing away of the landscape water
It is the wearing away of the landscape by ice

questions.explanation: The landscape is being continuously worn away by two processes - weathering and erosion. Weathering is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth’s surface.

questions.question: The process of erosion and deposition create-

options.option: Different landforms on the surface of the earth
Same landforms on the surface of the earth

questions.explanation: The landscape is being continuously worn away by two processes - weathering and erosion. Weathering is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth’s surface. Erosion is the wearing away of the landscape by different agents like water, wind and ice. The eroded material is carried away or transported by water, wind, etc. and eventually deposited. This process of erosion and deposition create different landforms on the surface of the earth.

questions.question: The highest waterfall is ________.
questions.explanation: The highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America.

questions.question: As the river approaches the sea, the speed of the flowing water decreases and the river begins to break up into a number of streams called ________.
questions.explanation: As the river approaches the sea, the speed of the flowing water decreases and the river begins to break up into a number of streams called distributaries. A distributary is a stream that branches off and flows away from a mainstream channel. They are a common feature of river deltas. The phenomenon is known as river bifurcation. The opposite of a distributary is a tributary.

questions.question: The erosion and deposition of the sea waves give rise to:
questions.explanation: The erosion and deposition of the sea waves gives rise to coastal landforms. Seawaves continuously strike at the rocks. Cracks develop. Over time they become larger and wider. Thus, hollow like caves are formed on the rocks.

questions.question: Glaciers are "rivers" of ________.
questions.explanation: Glaciers are “rivers of ice” which to erode the landscape by bulldozing soil and stones to expose the solid rock below. A glacier is a persistent body of dense ice that is constantly moving under its own weight; it forms where the accumulation of snow exceeds its ablation (melting and sublimation) over many years, often centuries.

questions.question: The material carried by the glacier such as rocks big and small, sand and silt gets deposited. These deposits form ________.
questions.explanation: Glaciers carve out deep hollows there. As the ice melts they get filled up with water and become beautiful lakes in the mountains. The material carried by the glacier such as rocks big and small, sand and silt gets deposited. These deposits form glacial moraines.

questions.question: Which of the following is the highest waterfall in India?
questions.explanation: The Jog waterfalls created by the Sharavathi river in Sharavathi valley of Shimoga district in Karnataka. Its breathtaking spectacle when Sharavathi river falling from a height of 829 ft.

questions.question: In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom. Explain why?

options.option: Because winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part
Because winds erode the lower section of the rock equally with the upper part
Because winds erode the lower section of the rock less than the upper part
Because water erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part

questions.explanation: An active agent of erosion and deposition in the deserts is wind. In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom, commonly called mushroom rocks. Winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part. Therefore, such rocks have a narrower base and wider top which look like mushrooms.

questions.question: When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called ________.
questions.explanation: Loess is a geologically recent deposit of silt or material which is usually yellowish or brown in color and consisting of tiny mineral particles brought by wind to the places where they now lie. When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called Loess.

questions.question: Which waterfall of the following is situated in North America?
questions.explanation: The highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America. The other waterfalls are Niagara falls located on the border between Canada and the USA in North America.

questions.question: Which is not an erosional feature of sea waves?
questions.explanation: Beach

questions.question: The depositional feature of a glacier is
questions.explanation: Moraine

questions.question: Mushroom rocks are found in
questions.explanation: Deserts

questions.question: Ox bow lakes are found in
questions.explanation: River valleys

questions.question: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of ________.
questions.explanation: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of air called atmosphere. All living beings on this earth depend on the atmosphere for their survival. It provides us the air we breathe and protects us from the harmful effects of the sun’s rays. Without this blanket of protection, we would be baked alive by the heat of the sun during the day and get frozen during the night. So it is this mass of air that has made the temperature on the earth liveable.

questions.question: Which two gases make up the bulk of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Our atmosphere is made up of around 78% of nitrogen and around 21% of oxygen.

questions.question: Which is the second most plentiful gas in the air?
questions.explanation: Around 21% of the atmosphere is made up of oxygen. Green plants produce oxygen during photosynthesis and humans and animals take oxygen from the air as they breathe.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the layer of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Hydrosphere refers to all the waters on the earth's surface, such as lakes and seas, and sometimes including water near the earth's surface, such as clouds. It is not a layer of the atmosphere.

questions.question: All the weather phenomena like rainfall, fog and hailstorm occur in which layer?
questions.explanation: The troposphere is the nearest layer to the earth's surface. Its height is 13 km. Hence, all weather phenomena like fog, hailstorm, and rainfall occur in this layer.

questions.question: Which layer is almost free from clouds?
questions.explanation: Above the troposphere, lies the stratosphere. Its height is 50 km. This layer is free from clouds and weather phenomena. It is ideal for flying aeroplanes.

questions.question: The air we breathe exists in ________.
questions.explanation: As Troposphere is nearest to the earth's surface and its height is 13 km, it contains the air we breathe.

questions.question: Meteorites burn up in this layer on entering from the space-
questions.explanation: This is caused by the meteorite rubbing against molecules in the mesosphere. When the friction is great enough, the meteorite burns up.

questions.question: Which of the following instruments is used to measure rainfall?
questions.explanation: A rain gauge (also known as an udometer, pluviometer, or an ombrometer) is an instrument used by meteorologists and hydrologists to gather and measure the amount of liquid precipitation over a set period of time. Most standard rain gauges have a wide funnel leading into the cylinder and are calibrated so that one-tenth of an inch of rain measures one inch when it collects inside. The funnel is 10 times the cross-sectional area of the tube. Rainfall as low as .01 inches can be measured with this instrument.

questions.question: The degree of hotness and coldness of the air is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Air temperature is a measure of how hot or cold the air is. It is the most commonly measured weather parameter. More specifically, temperature describes the kinetic energy, or energy of motion, of the gases that make up air. Air temperature is usually measured in Celsius (C) or Fahrenheit (F).

questions.question: Composition of air:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen accounts for 78% of the atmosphere, oxygen 21%, and argon 0.9%. Gases like carbon dioxide, nitrous oxides, methane, and ozone are trace gases that account for about a tenth of one percent of the atmosphere.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a type of rainfall?
questions.explanation: On the basis of mechanism, there are three types of rainfall: the convectional rainfall, the orographic rainfall and the cyclonic rainfall.

questions.question: What is released in the air from the burning of fuels?
questions.explanation: The burning of fuels such as gasoline, coal, oil, natural gas in combustion reactions results in the production of carbon dioxide. For example, One molecule of methane, combined with two oxygen molecules, reacts to form a carbon dioxide molecule, and two water molecules usually are given off as steam or water vapor during the reaction and energy.

questions.question: Why do astronauts wear special protective space suits?

options.option: If they did not wear these space suits, the counter-pressure exerted by the body of the astronauts would make the blood vessels burst. The astronauts would bleed

questions.explanation: There is no air pressure in outer space. Hence, the body pressure would exert itself and the blood vessels of astronauts would burst. Spacesuits are made of specific materials that maintain the temperature and protect the astronauts from the external pressure difference.

questions.question: Carbon dioxide released in the atmosphere creates:
questions.explanation: A greenhouse gas is a gas in an atmosphere that absorbs and emits radiation within the thermal infrared range. This process is the fundamental cause of the greenhouse effect which causes the earth's warming. The primary greenhouse gases in Earth's atmosphere are water vapor, carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, and ozone.

questions.question: When precipitation comes down to the earth in the liquid form, it is called:
questions.explanation: Rain is liquid water in the form of droplets that have condensed from atmospheric water vapour and then precipitated—that is, become heavy enough to fall under gravity. Rain is a major component of the water cycle and is responsible for depositing most of the fresh water on the Earth.

questions.question: Which of the following gas is found in a large quantity in the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: By volume, dry air contains 78.09% nitrogen, 20.95% oxygen, 0.93% argon, 0.04% carbon dioxide, and small amounts of other gases. So, nitrogen is the most abundant gas in the atmosphere.

questions.question: Which gas is needed by plants for their survival?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen is a component of chlorophyll and therefore essential for photosynthesis. It is also the basic element of plant and animal proteins, including the genetic material DNA and RNA, and is important in periods of rapid growth. Plants use nitrogen by absorbing either nitrate or ammonium ions through the roots.

questions.question: Which effect give rise in temperature causing the snow in coldest parts of the world to melt?
questions.explanation: Global warming refers to a gradual increase in the overall temperature of the earth's atmosphere generally attributed to the greenhouse effect caused by increased levels of carbon dioxide, CFCs, and other pollutants. This has led to a lot of glaciers melting at a faster rate near the North and South Poles.

questions.question: Who provides nitrogen to the plants?
questions.explanation: Plants are in a symbiotic relationship with microorganisms that are capable of nitrogen fixation from the air. They contain symbiotic bacteria called rhizobia within nodules in their root systems, producing nitrogen compounds that help the plant to grow and compete with other plants. When the plant dies, the fixed nitrogen is released, making it available to other plants; this helps to fertilize the soil.

questions.question: Green plants produce which gas during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation:
Green plants produce oxygen during photosynthesis. They use carbon dioxide and water and give out oxygen and create glucose. Photosynthesis can be represented using a chemical equation.
The overall balanced equation is: 6CO2 + 6H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C6H12O6 + 6O2

questions.question: In which layer the temperature rises very rapidly with increasing height?
questions.explanation: Taking its name from the Greek θερμός (pronounced thermos) meaning heat, the thermosphere begins about 85 kilometres (53 mi) above the Earth. At these high altitudes, the residual atmospheric gases sort into strata according to molecular mass. Thermospheric temperatures increase with altitude due to absorption of highly energetic solar radiation. Temperatures are highly dependent on solar activity, and can rise to 2,000 °C (3,630 °F).

questions.question: This layer helps in radio transmission. In fact, radio waves transmitted from the earth are reflected back to the earth by ________.
questions.explanation: Solar radiation causes the atmosphere particles in the thermosphere to become electrically charged. The high temperatures in the thermosphere can cause molecules to ionize, thus, enabling radio waves to be refracted and be received beyond the horizon.

questions.question: An instrument used for measuring atmospheric pressure is:
questions.explanation: Barometer is the instrument used to measure atmospheric pressure. A barometer is a scientific instrument used in meteorology to measure atmospheric pressure. Pressure tendency can forecast short term changes in the weather. The standard atmosphere (symbol: atm) is a unit of pressure defined as 101325 Pa (1.01325 bar), equivalent to 760 mmHg (torr), 29.92 in Hg and 14.696 psi. Please find below the diagram of a mercury barometer.

questions.question: Insolation stands for:
questions.explanation: Insolation refers to the amount of solar radiation reaching a given area. It is measured by the amount of solar energy received per square centimetre per minute.

questions.question: The cyclone hit Odisha in ________.
questions.explanation:
The 1999 Odisha cyclone was the strongest recorded tropical cyclone in the North Indian Ocean and among the most destructive in the region. The 1999 Odisha cyclone organized into a tropical depression in the Andaman Sea on 25 October, though its origins could be traced back to an area of storms in the Sulu Sea four days prior.
The cyclone's impacts were very severe. The 5-6 m (16-20 ft) surge brought water up to 35 km (20 mi) inland, carrying along with it coastal debris and inundating towns and villages. The surge combined with heavy rains to produce widespread flooding, damaging around 1.6 million homes and causing rivers to breach 20,005 flood embankments. The storm's effects destroyed numerous crops, including sugar cane, rice, and other winter-time harvests. Although estimates of the death toll varied significantly-at times suggesting 30,000 fatalities-the Government of India enumerated 9,887 fatalities in the country, of which a majority were caused by storm surge. The total damage cost of the destruction wrought by the super cyclone amounted to US$4.44 billion.

questions.question: The standard unit of measuring temperature is degree Celsius. It was invented by ________.
questions.explanation: Anders Celsius is famous for inventing the temperature scale, which he based on the boiling and melting points of water. This scale, an inverted form of Celsius' original design, was adopted as the standard and is used in almost all scientific work. The Celsius temperature scale is also referred to as the centigrade scale. Centigrade means "consisting of or divided into 100 degrees". The Celsius scale, invented by Swedish Astronomer Anders Celsius (1701-1744), has 100 degrees between the freezing point (0 C) and boiling point (100 C) of pure water at sea level air pressure.

questions.question: What is the difference in temperature of cities and in villages?

options.option: In village, the temperature is equal to cities
In cities, the temperature is higher than in villages
In villages, the temperature is higher than in cities

questions.explanation:
Temperature is higher in cities than in villages because

  1. Cities are densely populated.
  2. There is more traffic in the cities.
  3. There is more industrial growth.

All these factors lead to more pollution and an increase in temperatures in cities than in villages.

questions.question: On which day do clothes take longer to dry and sweat from our body does not evaporate easily, making us feel very uncomfortable?
questions.explanation: On a humid day, the atmosphere air contains more water vapour than on a dry day and hence evaporation is slower. So, clothes take longer to dry. On humid days, when the air is already saturated with water, sweat evaporates more slowly. This explains why it feels so much hotter in humidity.

questions.question: Which gas is found in large quantity in the air?
questions.explanation: By volume, dry air contains 78.08% nitrogen, 20.95% oxygen, 0.93% argon, 0.04% carbon dioxide, and small amounts of other gases. Air also contains a variable amount of water vapor, on average around 1% at sea level, and 0.4% over the entire atmosphere. Hence, Nitrogen gas is the most abundant in air.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the effect of global warming?

options.option: Helps in securing from harmful sun rays
Rise in temperature causes the snow in coldest parts of the world to melt
The sea level rises, causing floods in the coastal areas
There may be drastic changes in the climate of a place leading to extinction of some plants and animals in the long run

questions.explanation: Global warming refers to a gradual increase in the overall temperature of the earth's atmosphere generally attributed to the greenhouse effect caused by increased levels of carbon dioxide, CFCs, and other pollutants. Hence, global warming increases the overall temperature and does not protect or secure the earth and its inhabitants from harmful rays.

questions.question: Which of the following gases protects us from harmful sun vays?
questions.explanation: Ozone

questions.question: The most important layer of the atmosphere is
questions.explanation: Troposphere

questions.question: Which of the following layers of the atmosphere is free from clouds?
questions.explanation: Above the troposphere lies the stratosphere. This layer is almost free from clouds and associated weather phenomenon, making conditions most ideal for flying aeroplanes.

questions.question: As we go up the layers of the atmosphere, the pressure
questions.explanation: Atmospheric pressure decreases as the height of a surface above ground level increases. As we go up the layers of the atmosphere, fewer air molecules are above us. So, there will be less weight of air above us. Hence, atmospheric pressure always decreases as we move upward in the atmosphere.

questions.question: The major sources of fresh water are as follows except:
questions.explanation: Rivers, ponds, and glaciers are sources of freshwater. But oceans don't have fresh water.

questions.question: Which is the largest ocean?
questions.explanation: Pacific ocean is the largest ocean in the world.

questions.question: It is an artificial enclosure for keeping small house plants:
questions.explanation: A terrarium (plural: terraria or terrariums) is usually a sealable glass container containing soil and plants. Terraria are often kept as decorative or ornamental items.

questions.question: How much percentage of saline water is available on the earth's surface?
questions.explanation: 97.3% of saline water is available on the earth's surface.

questions.question: A dead sea in Israel has a salinity of
questions.explanation: A dead sea in Israel has a salinity of 340 grams per litre of water. Swimmers can float in it because the increased salt content makes it dense.

questions.question: Following movements occur in oceans except for the movement of ________.
questions.explanation: Waves, currents, and tides are different movements of the ocean. But not the wind.

questions.question: ________ are formed when winds scrape across the ocean surface.
questions.explanation: Waves are formed when winds scrape across the ocean surface.

questions.question: Indira point was in ________.
questions.explanation: Indira Point was a village in the Nicobar district at Great Nicobar Island of Andaman and Nicobar Islands, India. It was located in the Great Nicobar tehsil. It was the location of the southernmost point of India's territory.

questions.question: The rhythmic rise and fall of ocean water twice in a day is called a
questions.explanation: The rhythmic rise and fall of ocean water twice in a day is called a tide.

questions.question: Water bodies contain salty water are
questions.explanation: Ocean and sea have salty water, other water bodies have fresh water

questions.question: Common salt that we mostly eat:
questions.explanation: Common salt that we use is sodium chloride- NaCl.

questions.question: The average salinity of the oceans is ________.
questions.explanation: On average, seawater in the world's oceans has a salinity of approximately 3.5%, or 35 parts per thousand. This means that for every 1 litre (1000 mL) of seawater there are 35 grams of salts (mostly, but not entirely, sodium chloride) dissolved in it.

questions.question: Dead sea is in ________.
questions.explanation: The Dead Sea - bordering Israel, the West Bank, and Jordan - is a salt lake whose banks are more than 400m below sea level, the lowest point on dry land. Its famously hypersaline water makes floating easy, and its mineral-rich black mud is used for therapeutic and cosmetic treatments at area resorts.

questions.question: When the water on the surface of the ocean rises and falls alternately, they are called ________.
questions.explanation: When the water on the surface of the ocean rises and falls alternately, they are called waves.

questions.question: Tsunami means:
questions.explanation: Tsunami, also called harbour wave rdquo; in Japanese (called seismic sea wave or tidal wave, catastrophic ocean wave), usually caused by a submarine earthquake, by an underwater or coastal landslide, or by the eruption of a volcano.

questions.question: High Tsunami waves travel at the speed of
questions.explanation: High tsunami waves travel at the speed of more than 700 km per hour.

questions.question: Which part of Andaman and Nicobar island submerged after the tsunami?
questions.explanation: Indira point of Andaman and Nicobar island submerged after tsunami.

questions.question: Tsunami or the harbour wave struck havoc in the Indian Ocean on the 26 December 2004. The wave was the result of the earthquake that had its epicenter close to the western boundary of ________.
questions.explanation: Tsunami or the harbour wave struck havoc in the Indian Ocean on the 26 December 2004. The wave was the result of the earthquake that had its epicenter close to the western boundary of Sumatra.

questions.question: The water of the earth closer to the moon gets pulled under the influence of the moon's gravitational force and causes ________.
questions.explanation: The water of the earth closer to the moon gets pulled under the influence of the moon's gravitational force and causes.

questions.question: Following are the importance of tides except:

options.option: It helps in protection from sun harmful rays

questions.explanation: Tides don't help in protection from sun rays. Rest all are the importance of tides.

questions.question: Warm ocean currents originate near
questions.explanation: Warm ocean currents originate near equator.

questions.question: How waves are differ from tides?

options.option: Waves occur 10 hours a day while tides occur two times a day
Waves occurs 24 hours a day while tides occur thrice times a day
Waves occurs 10 hours a day while tides occur thrice times a day
Waves occur 24 hours a day while tides occur two times a day

questions.explanation: Tides are formed because of the interaction of the gravitational forces between the Earth, the moon, and the sun. Waves are formed because of the gusting or raging force exerted by the wind on the water surface. Thus, waves occur 24 hrs a day while tides occur only twice a day.

questions.question: Which area provides the best fishing grounds of the world?

options.option: The areas where the warm and cold currents meets

questions.explanation: Best fishing areas are where warm and cold currents meet.

questions.question: Example of the areas where a warm and cold current meet are

options.option: Sea area around Japan and North America
Sea area around China and North America
Sea area around China and South America
Sea area around Japan and South America

questions.explanation:
Sea area around Japan and North America. i.e. The Labrador Current meets the Gulf Stream off the coast of Newfoundland. When warm, moist air from the Gulf Stream blows over the cold Labrador Current, water vapour condenses. This results in some of the thickest fogs in the world.
Can be understood with the help of the map below:

questions.question: Peru current, West wind drift, Oyashio current are ________.
questions.explanation: Peru current, West wind drift, Oyashio current are examples of cold current.

questions.question: Tsunami is a Japanese word that means “Harbour waves”. Why this name is given to tsunami?

options.option: Roads get destroyed whenever there is a tsunami
Ships get destroyed whenever there is a tsunami
Buildings get destroyed whenever there is a tsunami
Harbours get destroyed whenever there is a tsunami

questions.explanation: Tsunami is a Japanese word that means “Harbour waves” as the harbours get destroyed whenever there is a tsunami.

questions.question: Neap tides occur during:

options.option: Full moon and last quarter of moon
First quarter and last quarter of moon

questions.explanation: A tide just after the first or third quarters of the moon when there is the least difference between high and low water is neap tide. Neap tides occur twice a month when the sun and moon are at right angles to the Earth. When this is the case, their total gravitational pull on the Earth's water is weakened because it comes from two different directions.

questions.question: The Dead Sea in Israel has a salinity of 33.7 parts per thousand. Swimmers can float in it because

options.option: the decrease salt content makes it dense
the increased salt content makes it lighter
the increased salt content makes it dense
the decreased salt content makes it lighter

questions.explanation: The Dead Sea in Israel has a salinity of 33.7 parts per thousand. Swimmers can float in it because the increased salt content makes it dense.

questions.question: What is the percentage of fresh water?
questions.explanation: The percentage of freshwater on the earth surface is only 2.7%.

questions.question: Out of total water present on earth,0.68 percentage of water is in ________.
questions.explanation: 0.68 percentage of water is in groundwater.

questions.question: How much percent of the water available in rivers and freshwater lakes?
questions.explanation: Percent of water available in rivers and freshwater lakes is 0.0091

questions.question: The process by which water continually changes its form and circulates between ocean, atmosphere and land.
questions.explanation: Water cycle

chapters.name: Human Environment Interactions the Tropical and the Subtropical Region

questions.question: The Amazon Basin is the largest river basin in the ________.
questions.explanation: Numerous tributaries join the Amazon River to form the Amazon basin. The river basin drains portions of Brazil, parts of Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Columbia, and a small part of Venezuela. The main river along with all its tributaries that drain an area form a river basin or the catchment area. The Amazon Basin is the largest river basin in the world.

questions.question: The Amazon Basin is in ________.
questions.explanation: The main river along with all its tributaries that drain an area form a river basin or the catchment area. The Amazon Basin is the largest river basin in the world. Numerous tributaries join the Amazon River to form the Amazon basin. The river basin drains portions of Brazil, parts of Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Columbia, and a small part of Venezuela. The Amazon Basin is in South America.

questions.question: On The Amazon basin, days and nights are almost:
questions.explanation: The Amazon Basin stretches directly on the equator and is characterized by hot and wet climate throughout the year. Both day and nights are almost equally hot and humid. The skin feels sticky. It rains almost everyday, that too without much warning. The day temperatures are high with very high humidity. At night the temperature goes down but the humidity remains high.

questions.question: ________ are special plants that store water in their leaves.
questions.explanation: Rain forests are in fact so thick that the dense “roof” created by leaves and branches does not allow the sunlight to reach the ground. The ground remains dark and damp. Only shade-tolerant vegetation may grow here. Orchids, bromeliads grow as plant parasites in these forests. Bromeliads are special plants that store water in their leaves. Animals like frogs use these pockets of water for laying their eggs.

questions.question: In the rainforest how the work is divided among the men and women?

options.option: Women hunt and fish along the rivers, men take care of the crops
Women make pottery and man hunt and fish along the rivers
Men hunt and fish along the rivers, women take care of the crops
Men do cultivation along the rivers, women take care of the crops

questions.explanation: People of the rain forest grow most of their food in small areas after clearing some trees in the forest. While men hunt and fish along the rivers, women take care of the crops. They mainly grow tapioca, pineapple, and sweet potato. As hunting and fishing are uncertain it is the women who keep their families alive by feeding them the vegetables they grow. They practice “slash and burn agriculture”.

questions.question: The staple food in the rainforest area is ________.
questions.explanation: People in the rainforest grow most of their food in small areas after clearing some trees in the forest. While men hunt and fish along the rivers, women take care of the crops. They mainly grow tapioca, pineapple, and sweet potato. They practice “slash and burn agriculture”. The staple food is manioc, also known as cassava that grows under the ground like the potato. They also eat queen ants and egg sacs. Cash crops like coffee, maize, and cocoa are also grown.

questions.question: ________ is a way of cultivating land where farmers clear a piece of land by slashing or cutting down trees and bushes.
questions.explanation: Slash and Burn is a way of cultivating land where farmers clear a piece of land by slashing or cutting down trees and bushes. These are then burnt, which releases the nutrients into the soil. Now crops are grown in this cleared field for a few years. After repeatedly using the patch of land, the soil loses its nutrients. So it is abandoned. Then they clear another plot of land to plant. In the meantime, young trees grow in the old field. In this way, soil fertility is restored. People can then return to it and start cultivating it again.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the cash crop grown by the people living in Rainforests?
questions.explanation: People of the Rainforest grow most of their food in small areas after clearing some trees in the forest. While men hunt and fish along the rivers, women take care of the crops. They mainly grow tapioca, pineapple, and sweet potato. They practice “slash and burn agriculture”. The staple food is manioc, also known as cassava that grows under the ground like the potato. They also eat queen ants and egg sacs. Cash crops like coffee, maize, and cocoa are also grown by them.

questions.question: The Taj Mahal is located in ________.
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is an ivory-white marble mausoleum on the south bank of the Yamuna river in the Indian city of Agra. It was commissioned in 1632 by the Mughal emperor, Shah Jahan.

questions.question: Buddhist Stupa is situated in which states on the Basin of Ganga-Brahmaputra river?
questions.explanation: Tourism is another important activity of the Ganga-Brahmaputra basin. Buddhist stupas in Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, Lucknow with its Imambara, Taj Mahal on the banks of River Yamuna in Agra, Assam with Kaziranga and Manas wildlife sanctuaries and Arunachal Pradesh with a distinct tribal culture are some of the places situated near this river basin.

questions.question: Following are the tributaries of the River Ganga except:
questions.explanation: The Ganges or Ganga is a trans-boundary river of Asia which flows through the nations of India and Bangladesh. The 2,525 km long river rises in the eastern Himalayas in the Indian state of Uttarakhand and flows south and east through the Gangetic Plain of North India into Bangladesh, where it empties into the Bay of Bengal. The main tributaries of the River Ganga are the Ghaghra, the Son, the Chambal, the Gandak, the Kosi.

questions.question: In which of the following area of Ganga-Brahmaputra basin tea is grown in plantations?
questions.explanation: The Ganga-Brahmaputra basin is in the Indian subcontinent. The basin lies in the sub-tropical region. Agriculture is the main occupation of the people. In West Bengal and Assam tea is grown in plantations.

questions.question: Following are the tropical deciduous trees grow in the area of Ganga-Brahmaputra basin except:
questions.explanation: In the Ganga and Brahmaputra plain tropical deciduous trees grow, along with teak, sal, and peepal. Thick bamboo groves are common in the Brahmaputra plain. The delta area is covered with mangrove forests. In parts of Uttaranchal, Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh, coniferous trees like pine, deodar and fir can be seen because the climate is cool and the slopes are steep.

questions.question: The one-horned rhinoceros is found in the ________.
questions.explanation: There is a variety of wildlife in the basin. Elephants, tigers, deer, and monkeys are common. The one-horned rhinoceros is found in the Brahmaputra plain. In the delta area, Bengal tiger, crocodiles, and alligators are found.

questions.question: The Ganga-Brahmaputra plain has several big towns and cities except for _______.
questions.explanation: The tributaries of rivers Ganga and Brahmaputra together form the Ganga-Brahmaputra basin in the Indian subcontinent. The Ganga-Brahmaputra plain has several big towns and cities. The cities of Allahabad, Kanpur, Varanasi, Lucknow, Patna, and Kolkata all with a population of more than ten lakhs are located along the River Ganga. Kerala is a state situated in southern India.

questions.question: Paddy is grown in ________.
questions.explanation: In the Ganga-Brahmaputra basin agriculture is the main occupation of the people where flat land is available to grow crops. The main crop is paddy. Since the cultivation of paddy requires sufficient water, it is grown in the areas where the amount of rainfall is high. Wheat, maize, sorghum, gram, and millets are the other crops that are grown. Cash crops like sugarcane and jute are also grown. Banana plantations are seen in some areas of the plain. In West Bengal and Assam tea is grown in plantations.

subtopics.name: Growing up in Samoa in the 1920s

questions.question: Which was a very important activity on the Samoan islands?
questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Fishing was a very important activity on the islands. Young people, therefore, learnt to undertake long fishing expeditions.

questions.question: What is the sense of self-awareness?
questions.explanation: Being a boy or a girl is an important part of one’s identity. The society we grow up in teaches us what kind of behaviour is acceptable for girls and boys, what boys and girls can or cannot do. Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is. Typically, a person can have several identities. For example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.

questions.question: What is the term that is commonly used to describe the women's work and situation?
questions.explanation: Double-burden Literally means a double load. This term is commonly used to describe the women’s work situation. It has emerged from a recognition that women typically labour both inside the home (housework) and outside. In most societies, including our own, the roles men and women play or the work they do, are not valued equally. Men and women do not have the same status. If we add up the housework and the work, women do outside the home, we find that women spend much more time working than men and have much less time for leisure.

questions.question: Besides physical tasks, Women also involve a strong emotional aspect that is-
questions.explanation: Across the world, the main responsibility of a women is  housework and care-giving tasks, like looking after the family, especially children, the elderly and sick members. Yet, as we have seen, the work that women do within the home is not recognised as work. It is also assumed that this is something that comes naturally to women. It, therefore, does not have to be paid for. And society devalues this work.

questions.question: When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done, they can feel _____
questions.explanation: Societies assign different values to the roles men and women play and the work they do, which becomes a basis for inequality and discrimination. Work Done primarily by women as housework is often not considered ‘work’ and, therefore they feel invisible and self devalued.

questions.question: Were Harmeet and Shonali correct in saying that their mother do not work?

options.option: No, Harmeet mothers is elder than him
No, as household work is devalued
Yes, Her mother not do any work
Yes, as household work is revalued

questions.explanation: Harmeet’s family did not think that the work Jaspreet did within the house was real work. This feeling is not unique to their families. Across the world, the main responsibility for housework and care-giving tasks, like looking after the family, especially children, the elderly and sick members, lies with women. Yet, as we have seen, the work that women do within the home is not recognised as work. It is also assumed that this is something that comes naturally to women. It, therefore, does not have to be paid for. And society devalues this work.

subtopics.name: Growing up male in Madhya Pradesh in the 1960s

questions.question: It is a social construct and cannot be determined by biological differences-
questions.explanation: Gender is a term that you may often have heard. It is a term, however, that is not easily understood. It determines, for example, who we are and what we will become, where we can go and where not, the life choices available to us and those we eventually make. Our understanding of gender is often based on the family and society that we live in. This leads us to think that the roles we see men and women around us play are fixed and natural. In fact, these roles differ across communities around the world. By gender, then, we mean the many social values and stereotypes our cultures attach to the biological distinction ‘male’ and ‘female’. It is a term that helps us to understand many of the inequalities and power relations between men and women in society.

questions.question: At what age did the boys of Samoan join older boys for learning outdoor jobs?
questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Fishing was a very important activity on the islands. Both boys and girls looked after their younger siblings. But, by the time a boy was about 9 years old, he joined the older boys in learning outdoor jobs like fishing and planting coconuts.

questions.question: From which class did the girls and boys go to separate school in a town in Madhya Pradesh.
questions.explanation: On an account of experiences of being in a small town in Madhya Pradesh in the 1960s, From Class VI onwards, boys and girls went to separate schools. The girls’ school was designed very differently from the boys’ school. They had a central courtyard where they played in total seclusion and safety from the outside world. The boys’ school had no such courtyard and our playground was just a big space attached to the school.

questions.question: Who was Melani?
questions.explanation: Melani was a domestic worker who narrate about her experience of working in Delhi as– A domestic worker’s day can begin as early as five in the morning and end as late as twelve at night.Despite the hard work they do, their employers often do not show them much respect. This is what “My first job was with a rich family that lived in a three-storeyed house. The memsahib was very strange as she would shout to get any work done. My work was in the kitchen. There were two other girls who did the cleaning. Our day would begin at 5 o’clock.

questions.question: The government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than ____ employees to provide creche facilities.
questions.explanation: The government has set up anganwadis or child-care centres in several villages in the country. The government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than 30 women employees to provide crèche facilities. The provision of crèches helps many women to take up employment outside the home. It also makes it possible for more girls to attend schools.

questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Fishing was a very important activity on the islands.

questions.explanation: Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is. Typically, a person can have several identities. For example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.

questions.question: What did girls learn after the age of 14 years?
questions.explanation: Girls had to continue looking after small children or do errands for adults till they were teenagers. But, once they became teenagers they had much more freedom. After the age of fourteen or so, girls also went on fishing trips, worked in the plantations, learnt how to weave baskets. Cooking was done in special cooking-houses, where boys were supposed to do most of the work while girls helped with the preparations.

Men's work should be valued more than women's
Both of their works should not be valued at all

questions.explanation: In most societies, including our own, the roles men and women play or the work they do, are not valued equally. Men and women do not have the same status. The roles women play and the work they do are usually valued less than the roles men play and the work they do. This should not be encouraged. Both their work should be equally valued.

questions.question: When we do not treat people equally we create _________.
questions.explanation: When we do not treat people equally or with respect we are indulging in discrimination. It happens when people or organisations act on their prejudices. Discrimination usually takes place when we treat some one differently or make a distinction.

questions.question: Indian woman who wrote first autobiography?
questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography in Bangla. Her book titled Amar Jiban is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman. Rashsundari Devi was a housewife from a rich landlord’s family. At that time, it was believed that if a woman learnt to read and write, she would bring bad luck to her husband and become a widow! Despite this, she taught herself how to read and write in secret, well after her marriage.

In these markets, buying and selling takes place between traders. It is through these links of traders that goods reach faraway places. The trader who finally sells this to the consumer, is the retailer.

questions.question: Order of Chain of marketing
questions.explanation:
A series of markets that are connected like links in a chain because products pass from one market to another.

PRODUCERS

Producer is a person, company, or country that makes, grows, or supplies goods or commodities for sale

WHOLESALE TRADER
A person who buys products in bulk from factory and may store them in godown, and sell it to other traders.
AGENTS:
The people in between the producer and the final consumer are the traders.
RETAILER
The trader who buys good from the wholesaler and sells it to the consumer.All neighbourhood shops are retailers.

questions.question: What is linked in the chain market?
questions.explanation: Chain of Markets refers to the series of exchange of goods that takes place from the moment the goods are produced to the time they reach the end user i.e. the final consumer. Goods are produced in factories, farms and homes. But we do not buy directly from factories or farms. Even the producers will not be willing to sell small quantities like one dress or one kilogram of wheat to the consumers. Hence traders come into the picture. Traders are the people in between the producer and the final consumer.

questions.question: What does the cultivation of cotton require?
questions.explanation: Cultivation of cotton requires high levels of inputs such as fertilisers and pesticides and the farmers have to incur heavy expenses on account of these. Most often, small farmers need to borrow money to meet these expenses.

questions.question: Who buys the bales of cotton to convert them into yarn?
